title: Emergency Access Procedure — Downtime / Break-Glass Account Usage
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the HIPAA Security programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with HIPAA Security control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: HIPAA Security §164.312(a)(2)(ii) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.312(a)(2)(ii) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.10.14: Emergency Access Procedure — Downtime / Break-Glass Account Usage.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.10.14' running on WinEventLog:Security 4624 for svc_epic_emergency*, index=epic sourcetype=epic:audit AccessContext="Downtime", archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Emergency Access Procedure — Downtime / Break-Glass Account ' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper HIPAA Security control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the HIPAA Security control objective.

app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Enterprise Security (263) (Splunkbase)
spl:
(index=windows EventCode="4622" OR EventCode="4624") Account_Name="svc_epic_emergency*" earliest=-30d
| append [
    search index=epic sourcetype="epic:audit" AccessContext="*" earliest=-30d
    | rex field=AccessContext "(?<ctx>Downtime|Emergency)"
    | where ctx IN ("Downtime","Emergency") ]
| eval principal=coalesce(Account_Name, USER_ID)
| stats earliest(_time) as first latest(_time) as last dc(host) as systems by principal, ctx
| eval duration_h=round((last-first)/3600,2)
| sort - last
description: Audits emergency and downtime accounts touching production ePHI so procedural safeguards around emergency access are reviewable and time-bound.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for HIPAA Security compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes epic, windows and sourcetypes epic:audit, epic:audit`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Require post-event attestation form ID in separate ticketing sourcetype and join; (2) auto-expire passwords via IAM; (3) weekly privacy review of all uses. Indexes required: `index=epic`, `index=windows`.
visualization: Table (principal, duration, systems), Single value (uses per month).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Break-the-glass emergency access** — Clinicians exercising documented emergency access procedures to ePHI during patient safety events generate elevated access alerts that are legitimate under the HIPAA treatment, payment, and operations (TPO) exception.

2. **Health information exchange (HIE) activity** — Automated ePHI transmissions through approved health information exchanges and interoperability interfaces produce cross-boundary data flows that are authorised under business associate agreements.

3. **Clinical research data extraction** — IRB-approved research protocols involve de-identified or limited data set extractions that trigger data access monitoring rules but are covered by the approved research data use agreement.

4. **System backup and archival processes** — Scheduled ePHI backup operations, media rotation, and long-term archival procedures generate bulk data access events that are operationally required and documented in the information lifecycle policy.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `hipaa_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved break-glass event IDs, HIE partner identifiers, IRB protocol numbers, and scheduled backup job identifiers. The correlation search filters against this lookup and suppresses matches where `hipaa_exception_approved=true`.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Epic Audit Log Integration (facility-specific TA) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Epic Audit Log Integration (facility-specific TA)" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `windows`, `epic` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `§164.312(a)(2)(ii)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that HIPAA Security §164.312(a)(2)(ii) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.10.14: Emergency Access Procedure — Downtime / Break-Glass Account Usage." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Epic Audit Log Integration (facility-specific TA) , landing events in `windows`, `epic` under `epic:audit`. Epic Hyperspace audit exports run 5000-50000 events/day depending on facility size; at 1 KB/event plan 5-50 GB/month per facility.

Configure the input through Universal Forwarder on the Epic audit export host; coordinate export schedule with Epic admin team. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[monitor:///opt/epic/audit/export/*.json]
index = epic
sourcetype = epic:audit
disabled = 0
crcSalt = <SOURCE>
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `USER_ID, PAT_ID, AccessType, AccessContext, WorkstationName, Department, _time`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=windows earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Require post-event attestation form ID in separate ticketing sourcetype and join. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** auto-expire passwords via IAM. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** weekly privacy review of all uses. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `earliest()`, `latest()`, `dc()` across the last 30 days, grouped by `principal, ctx`. For "Emergency Access Procedure — Downtime / Break-Glass Account Usage," the aggregation summarises access activity — the control is about WHO accessed WHAT, so the key fields in the aggregate map directly to 'principal' and 'resource' for the compliance narrative. `dc()` (distinct count) answers 'how many unique X' — use this when the control measures coverage or diversity, not raw activity volume. A million events from one user counts as 1 distinct user; this is usually the right compliance semantics. `earliest()` and `latest()` extract the first and last value per group — typically used to measure durations ('first seen' to 'last seen') or to pick the freshest attribute value when multiple events update the same entity.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `principal, ctx` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(principal) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.10.14: Emergency Access Procedure — Downtime / Break-Glass Account ]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.10.14,reg=HIPAA_Security
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=windows sourcetype=epic:audit earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `ctx`, `duration_h`, `first`, `last`, `principal`, `systems`. Run:

```spl
index=windows sourcetype=epic:audit earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table ctx, duration_h, first, last, principal, systems
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.10.14: HIPAA Security evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`principal`, `duration`, `systems`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`uses per month`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'HIPAA Security current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**HIPAA Security-specific failure modes:**

• Minimum-necessary principle: access-anomaly detections (excessive chart views, unexpected departments) must NOT flag clinicians accessing records for treatment purposes — §164.502(b) explicitly permits this. Join detections against the clinician's current department and patient's active encounter to avoid generating thousands of false positives on ED doctors seeing admitted patients.

• Break-glass and emergency access (§164.312(a)(2)(ii)): emergency accounts will legitimately bypass normal access controls during downtime events. Maintain a lookup `breakglass_declarations.csv` written by the clinical informatics on-call; correlate every break-glass access against a declared event — un-declared emergency access is the finding.

• Audit log retention (§164.312(b)): HIPAA requires 6 years of audit log retention, longer than most Splunk index default settings. Verify `frozenTimePeriodInSecs >= 189216000` for every index named in an HIPAA UC's search, and that the archive tier (S3, cold storage) is provisioned.

• Covered vs non-covered entity scope: if the search unintentionally pulls data from a non-covered business unit (e.g. a research arm not operating as a HIPAA Covered Entity), findings are noise. Filter using the facility/cost-center lookup that the Privacy Officer maintains, not by index name alone.
grandmaExplanation: We audits emergency and downtime accounts touching production ePHI so procedural safeguards around emergency access are reviewable and time-bound. HIPAA is about keeping health records private and secure when we use them in care and operations.
